enter.speaker: narrator
enter.text: The cabin is small and well-kept. Cords of wood stacked neat along the north wall. The old man on the porch does not stand as you approach. His axe is across his knees, oiled, resting.

orric-look.speaker: orric
orric-look.text: Stop where you are, a moment. Let me look at you. ...Aye. You've come from the guild. Brenna sent you, or the wall of names did. It amounts to the same.

orric-offer.speaker: orric
orric-offer.text: I'm Orric. These are my woods, for what that's worth — I've lived in them longer than the guild has had a door. Ask what you came to ask.

choices.text: I have the bone shards you need.
I have three wolf pelts. The one you are looking for is among them.
Tell me what the woods took from you.
You weren't always a forester, were you?
Have you seen an Elven scholar named Veyrin?
Brenna has lost a guild member. Hesta. Have you seen her?
What is wrong with the woods?

bone-ritual-offer.speaker: orric
bone-ritual-offer.text: There is a rite — old, from before the guild, before the town. It seals a path against what walks it. I need bone shards from the cairn. Three of them. The dead leave them behind when they fall, and the shards still carry the resonance of the place they died in.

bone-ritual-accept.speaker: orric
bone-ritual-accept.text: Bring them whole. Cracked ones lose the pitch. I will do the rest.

bone-ritual-turnin.speaker: orric
bone-ritual-turnin.text: Three shards. Whole, clean breaks. Good. I can work with these.

bone-ritual-turnin-2.speaker: narrator
bone-ritual-turnin-2.text: Orric takes the bone shards and arranges them in a line on the porch railing. He murmurs something in a language that is not quite speech — more like the sound a forest makes when the wind dies and the leaves still move.

bone-ritual-done.speaker: orric
bone-ritual-done.text: The eastern path will be quieter now. Not safe — nothing in these woods is safe. But quieter. Fifty-five gold, from my own stores. You have my thanks, which is worth less, but I give it anyway.

veyrin-seen.speaker: orric
veyrin-seen.text: Aye. I saw him. Paler than most elves, and he carried a book he'd tucked against his ribs like it might run. He sat where you're standing and asked me about the old cairn east of here — the one under the ivy, past the hollow oak.

veyrin-warning.speaker: orric
veyrin-warning.text: I told him to let it alone. Some things are sealed for reasons older than the people who sealed them. He smiled the polite elven smile and went east anyway. That was eleven days ago. He has not come back. The crows have been quieter since.

veyrin-next.speaker: orric
veyrin-next.text: If you mean to go after him, the path east of my cabin is the one he took. Step carefully. The woods know when a stranger is looking for something, and they do not always point the right way.

woods.speaker: orric
woods.text: Quieter than they should be. Fewer birds. The deer have moved off west. When the woods go quiet in a place, it is not because nothing is there. It is because something is listening.

passing.speaker: orric
passing.text: No one passes through Greenhollow just passing through. You'll either find what you came for or what was waiting. Walk softly either way. I'll be on this porch if you come back.

hire-orric.speaker: orric
hire-orric.text: Fifty gold. Fair enough for a man who knows these woods better than any wall-name guild does. I will not carry your pack. But I will keep you breathing.

hire-orric-confirm.speaker: orric
hire-orric-confirm.text: Stay close when the fighting starts. These old arms still swing true.

orric-backstory-1.speaker: orric
orric-backstory-1.text: I was A-rank once. Guild name: Stonewall. I walked away after Veyrin disappeared the first time — not this time, the time before. Twenty years ago. I told myself the forest needed watching. The truth is I needed to stop.

orric-backstory-1-close.speaker: orric
orric-backstory-1-close.text: Now you know. Do not tell Brenna. She has enough weight on her ledger without adding mine.

orric-backstory-2.speaker: orric
orric-backstory-2.text: Because I killed something in those depths that I should not have. It was not evil — it was lost. And I was too tired to tell the difference. That is when a man should put down his weapon.

orric-backstory-2-close.speaker: orric
orric-backstory-2-close.text: You are still young enough to see the difference. Hold on to that. It goes faster than you think.

orric-grown.speaker: orric
orric-grown.text: ...Aye. You stand differently now. The shoulders settled. The hands quiet. I have seen that change in maybe four people in my life. Two of them lived long enough to teach others. The other two did not.

orric-grown-2.speaker: orric
orric-grown-2.text: Do not let the change make you proud. The woods do not respect proud men. They tolerate the quiet ones a little longer. That is the most you can ask of them.

cloak-offer.speaker: narrator
cloak-offer.text: Orric does not answer at first. He sets the axe down across his knees, slowly, the way a man does when he is buying himself a moment. Then he reaches inside his coat and brings out a bundle of cloth — green wool, faded, the hem torn ragged. He lays it on the porch boards between you.

cloak-offer-2.speaker: orric
cloak-offer-2.text: My daughter wore this into Greenhollow nineteen years ago. The cloak came back six days later, tied to the throat of a wolf, like a flag. The wolf ran before I could put a shaft in it.

cloak-offer-3.speaker: orric
cloak-offer-3.text: I have seen it again. Older now. Slower. Still wearing what is left of it. I do not know which of the wolves out there it is, and my eyes are not what they were. Bring me three pelts. I will know the one. I will know it by the weight.

cloak-accept.speaker: orric
cloak-accept.text: Whole pelts. Not torn through the spine. The one that wore her cloth will smell of her, and the others will not. That is all I ask.

cloak-turnin.speaker: narrator
cloak-turnin.text: Orric takes the pelts in both hands, one at a time. He weighs them. He brings the second one to his face for a long moment, and his eyes close, and his shoulders go very still. When he opens his eyes again the porch is the same and the world is the same and he is not.

cloak-turnin-2.speaker: orric
cloak-turnin-2.text: It is this one. I am sure of it. ...Thank you. I will not say her name to you. She has been dead nineteen years and I have not earned the breath for it yet.

cloak-turnin-3.speaker: narrator
cloak-turnin-3.text: He carries the pelt and the cloak both around the back of the cabin. The smoke that rises a moment later is not the cookfire's. He returns with empty hands and the smell of wool burning on him.

cloak-turnin-4.speaker: orric
cloak-turnin-4.text: Two hundred and sixty gold from a tin I keep under the hearth. Three hundred and eighty of your experience, for what that is worth to a man your age. The rest of what I owe you cannot be paid in coin.

cloak-turnin-close.speaker: orric
cloak-turnin-close.text: Walk gently in my woods from now on. They are quieter than they were this morning. So am I.

orric-named-line.speaker: orric
orric-named-line.text: ...You walk like she did. Heel first, weight back. I had not noticed before. I notice now. I am sorry — that was not for you. I have not used a name for anyone in a long time. I will use yours, friend. Come and go as you please.

hesta-pointer.speaker: orric
hesta-pointer.text: Hesta. Aye. She came through the trees three nights running, slept in the bramble, would not knock. On the fourth morning I left the spare bed turned down and the door unbarred. She has been sweeping my porch since. She does not speak. She will speak to you, perhaps. The cabin is open. Walk in slow.
